test_description: This test case evaluates the system's ability to retrieve and synthesize information from multiple, separate document sections to answer a complex question.
question: [Fill] The _____ blank in the chemical–mechanical polishing (CMP) process model depends on both the pad compliance and the selectivity between metal and dielectric materials, and the model predicts that an increase in this parameter leads to greater dishing of metal lines, as shown by combining the pressure distribution analysis with the asperity shielding effect.

gold_context_sources.section_title: A model for chemical–mechanical polishing of a material surface based on contact mechanics
gold_context_sources.evidence: The model takes into account the compliance of the polishing pad as well as its roughness. ... It is shown that the pressure distribution between an asperity and the wafer surface is discontinuous at edges of features that have different nominal polish rates and that this pressure discontinuity dominates the polish rate and dishing of narrow features. ... Fig. 4 shows the dishing of a metal line as a function of time for various values of the selectivity. Dishing increases with increasing polish time and decreasing selectivity, and eventually reaches a steady state. According to the model, the amount of recess in the steady state is given by A=clns+ Aw, where s is the selectivity and Qwp the difference in pad deflection between the center and the edge of the metal line. ... Figs. 5a and b illustrate the effect of the plane-strain elastic modulus of the pad on the surface profile of the wafer. A more compliant pad clearly results in more dishing since the pad deflects more easily into recessed areas on the wafer. Fig. 5b shows that the metal recess in the steady state increases linearly with pad compliance. This observation agrees with Eq. (12) since the pad deflection Aw, is inversely proportional to pad stiffness.

gold_context_sources.section_title: A model for chemical–mechanical polishing of a material surface based on contact mechanics
gold_context_sources.evidence: The interaction of asperities with the edges of features patterned on the wafer surface and the effect on the surface profile will be explored in more detail in the next section. ... It is impossible for a discontinuous step to form within the context of this model. Any step that forms as a result of a discontinuity in wear coefficient would be removed immediately. This in turn implies that the pressure cannot be continuous across a boundary between two materials with different wear coefficients. According to Eq. (10), the ratio of the average pressure on either side of the boundary must be equal to the selectivity to ensure an equal removal rate for both materials and to keep a step from forming. The resulting contact can therefore not be described as a simple Hertzian contact. ... It is possible to couple the pad roughness model presented in Section 2 with the asperity shielding model discussed in this section. This can be achieved by using the pressure distribution obtained from the roughness model to determine the interaction of the asperities with the edges of features patterned on a wafer.

ground_truth_answer: The correct answer is selectivity. Reasoning: The CMP model described integrates the pad compliance and the selectivity parameter between metal and dielectric materials to predict the dishing phenomenon observed in metal lines. The pressure distribution at feature edges, influenced by asperity shielding, leads to discontinuities in nominal polish rates. The model (Eq. 12) explicitly relates steady-state metal recess (dishing) to both pad deflection (which depends on pad compliance) and selectivity. Increased selectivity leads to greater differential polishing rates, increasing dishing. Furthermore, asperity shielding effects enforce pressure ratios equal to selectivity at material boundaries, preventing step formation and further controlling surface evolution. Hence, the selectivity blank captures the critical combined effect of material-dependent removal rates and pad mechanical properties on CMP-induced dishing.
